This pattern reflects suppressed ADH during water loading, which keeps aquaporin-2 channels out of the collecting-duct membrane, so water is not reabsorbed and dilute urine is excreted. Only the water-load condition produces hypotonic urine.

distractors.why: Confuses high urine osmolality with being hypotonic; euhydrated urine (620) exceeds plasma (288), so it is hypertonic.
Dehydrated urine (1180) is the most concentrated and far exceeds plasma; it is the most hypertonic, not hypotonic.
Both of these have urine osmolality above plasma, so neither is hypotonic; only water load qualifies.

questions.stem: Why did the investigators measure GFR by inulin clearance and report that it did not differ across conditions?
options: To confirm that ADH secretion was suppressed during dehydration
To rule out changes in filtered load as the cause of the differences in urine output
To show that plasma osmolality was the dependent variable
To demonstrate that the collecting duct was the only nephron segment involved
questions.explanation: Urine output could change either because the kidney filters more or less fluid (filtered load, set by GFR) or because the tubules reabsorb more or less water. By showing GFR was statistically unchanged (p = 0.42), the investigators eliminated filtered load as a confound, supporting their conclusion that ADH-driven tubular water reabsorption caused the differences in urine flow. Inulin is freely filtered and neither reabsorbed nor secreted, making its clearance an ideal GFR marker. Holding GFR constant isolates the tubular mechanism, strengthening internal validity of the causal claim about ADH.

distractors.why: ADH rose with dehydration in Table 1; GFR measurement addresses filtration, not whether ADH was suppressed.
Constant GFR does not localize the effect to one segment; it only excludes a filtration-driven explanation.
Plasma osmolality was the manipulated/independent driver across conditions, not a dependent variable, and GFR does not establish that.

questions.stem: The progressive rise in plasma ADH from the water-load to the dehydrated condition is best explained by which mechanism?
options: Reduced aquaporin-2 insertion feeding back to the hypothalamus
Decreased plasma osmolality stimulating posterior pituitary release
Increased plasma osmolality stimulating hypothalamic osmoreceptors
Increased GFR raising filtered sodium delivery to the macula densa
questions.explanation: ADH release is governed primarily by hypothalamic osmoreceptors that sense rising plasma osmolality. As subjects moved from water load (281 mOsm/kg) to dehydration (297 mOsm/kg), increasing osmolality stimulated these osmoreceptors, driving greater posterior pituitary release of ADH (0.5 to 7.8 pg/mL). This is exactly the trend in Table 1, where ADH tracks osmolality. The hormone then increases collecting-duct water reabsorption, concentrating urine and helping restore normal osmolality. Higher, not lower, osmolality is the stimulus, and the pathway is osmoreceptor-driven rather than dependent on GFR or aquaporin feedback.

distractors.why: Reverses the direction: decreased osmolality suppresses ADH; the data show ADH rising as osmolality rises.
GFR was constant and the macula densa primarily regulates renin/tubuloglomerular feedback, not osmoreceptor-driven ADH release.
Aquaporin-2 insertion is a downstream effect of ADH, not the upstream signal that controls hypothalamic ADH secretion.

questions.stem: A feature of the study design that most strengthens the inference that ADH caused the changes in urine output is that:
options: Urine osmolality was measured in mOsm/kg
Subjects were healthy adults
Each subject served as their own control across conditions
The dehydrated condition lasted 18 hours
questions.explanation: Using a within-subject (repeated-measures) design, where each subject is tested in all three conditions, controls for stable individual differences such as baseline kidney function, body size, and genetics. This reduces between-subject variability and increases power to attribute observed differences to the manipulated variable (hydration/ADH) rather than to confounds that differ between people. Randomizing the order of conditions further limits order effects. While a healthy sample improves homogeneity, it does not control individual variation as directly. The measurement units and the specific dehydration duration are procedural details that do not, by themselves, strengthen the causal inference.

distractors.why: A healthy sample improves homogeneity and generalizability somewhat but does not control within-person confounds the way a paired design does.
Units of measurement are a reporting detail and do not affect the strength of a causal inference.
The 18-hour duration defines the condition but is not itself a control feature that isolates ADH's effect.

questions.stem: From the water-load to the dehydrated condition, urine flow rate fell sharply while urine osmolality rose steeply. Given that GFR was constant, this combination most directly reflects:
options: Increased glomerular filtration with constant reabsorption
Equal reabsorption of water and solute throughout the nephron
Reabsorption of water with comparatively less solute in the collecting duct
Net secretion of water into the tubular lumen
questions.explanation: Urine flow fell from 14.0 to 0.4 mL/min while urine osmolality rose from 65 to 1180 mOsm/kg. Concentrating the urine while shrinking its volume requires removing water from the filtrate faster than solute, so the remaining solute is dissolved in much less water. This is the signature of ADH action: aquaporin-2 channels let water exit the collecting duct down the osmotic gradient into the hypertonic medulla, while solute is largely retained in the lumen. Because GFR was held constant, a filtration-driven cause is excluded, pointing to selective tubular water reabsorption as the mechanism.

distractors.why: GFR was held constant in the study, so increased filtration cannot explain the change.
Equal water and solute reabsorption would keep urine isotonic and would not raise urine osmolality so steeply.
Net water secretion would dilute and increase urine volume, the opposite of the observed concentration and volume drop.

title: Allele Frequencies of a Metabolic Disorder
text:
Phenylketonuria-like metabolic disorders follow an autosomal recessive pattern, in which affected individuals are homozygous (aa) for a loss-of-function allele a, and heterozygous carriers (Aa) are clinically unaffected. Because carriers cannot be distinguished from homozygous dominant (AA) individuals by phenotype alone, population geneticists often estimate the carrier burden indirectly from the frequency of affected newborns, assuming the population is in Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium (HWE).

A research team studied a recessive metabolic disorder caused by a single locus across four geographically distinct island populations (Regions A-D) that have historically practiced little interbreeding with one another. In each region, the team screened 2,000 newborns using a validated enzyme assay that reliably identifies affected (aa) infants at birth. Carriers were not directly genotyped. For each region, the investigators recorded the number of affected newborns and used the affected frequency to estimate the recessive allele frequency (q) and the expected carrier frequency (2pq), assuming HWE held within each region (Table 1).

The team noted that the four island populations differed substantially in historical founder size and in rates of consanguineous (related-partner) marriage. To test whether allele frequencies were stable over time, the investigators repeated the screen in Region C a decade later and obtained an affected count statistically indistinguishable from the original. As a methodological check, they also pooled all 8,000 newborns into a single combined sample and recomputed an overall affected frequency, then compared the carrier frequency predicted from the pooled data against the average of the four region-specific carrier estimates.

The investigators emphasized that their carrier estimates depend on several HWE assumptions, including random mating, no selection at the locus, negligible migration between regions, and a large population size. They cautioned that violations of these assumptions in any single island could bias the inferred carrier frequency for that region (Figure 1).

table.caption: Table 1. Affected newborn counts and Hardy-Weinberg estimates by region (N = 2,000 newborns each).

questions.stem: Based on Table 1, approximately how many carriers (Aa) would be expected among the 2,000 newborns screened in Region D?
options: 1,280 carriers
320 carriers
960 carriers
720 carriers
questions.explanation: Carrier frequency in Region D is 2pq = 0.480. Multiplying by the sample size gives the expected number of carriers: 0.480 x 2,000 = 960 carriers, so the answer is 960. Choice A (320) is the number of affected aa individuals in Region D, not carriers. Choice B (720) corresponds to homozygous dominant AA individuals in Region D, since p = 0.60 gives p² = 0.36 and 0.36 x 2,000 = 720. Choice D (1,280) is the AA count for Region B (0.64 x 2,000), pulled from the wrong region. The key skill is reading the correct row (D) and column (2pq) and converting a frequency to a count by multiplying by N.

distractors.why: Reports the affected (aa) count, confusing carriers with homozygous recessives.
Gives the homozygous dominant (p² x N) count instead of the heterozygous (2pq x N) count.
Pulls the AA count from the wrong region (B), not carriers in Region D.

questions.stem: In Region B, the recessive allele frequency q is 0.20. What fraction of all copies of the recessive allele a in that population are carried by unaffected (heterozygous) individuals rather than by affected individuals?
questions.explanation: Heterozygotes carry one a allele each; affected aa individuals carry two each. Per individual, the genotype frequencies are 2pq = 0.320 (carriers) and q² = 0.040 (affected). Allele copies from carriers = 1 x 0.320 = 0.320; from affected = 2 x 0.040 = 0.080. Total a copies = 0.400, which equals 2q = 0.40, as expected. The fraction in carriers = 0.320 / 0.400 = 0.80. When q is small, most recessive alleles are hidden in heterozygous carriers, which is why rare recessive alleles persist in a population despite selection against affected homozygotes.

distractors.why: Simply restates q rather than computing the share of alleles held by heterozygotes.
Assumes alleles split evenly, ignoring that carriers vastly outnumber affected individuals.
Overestimates by mishandling the factor-of-2 weighting for aa individuals' two alleles.

questions.stem: The investigators pooled all 8,000 newborns and computed an overall affected frequency, then compared the carrier frequency predicted from the pooled data to the average of the four regional carrier estimates. This pooled-versus-regional comparison is best described as a test for which potential problem?
options: Genotyping error in the enzyme assay
The Wahlund effect, in which combining genetically distinct subpopulations distorts apparent Hardy-Weinberg proportions
Incomplete penetrance of the recessive allele
Sampling bias from unequal regional sample sizes
questions.explanation: The four islands have different allele frequencies and little interbreeding, so they are distinct subpopulations. Pooling them creates a deficit of homozygotes relative to single-population HWE expectations—the Wahlund effect. The pooled affected frequency is 600/8,000 = 0.075, giving q ≈ 0.274 and 2pq ≈ 0.398, which differs from the average regional carrier estimate (0.350). This discrepancy is the signature of hidden population structure, so the comparison tests exactly that. Genotyping error and incomplete penetrance concern phenotype measurement, not structure. Sampling bias from unequal sizes is ruled out because all four samples are equal (N = 2,000), so unequal sizes cannot explain the discrepancy.

distractors.why: Assay reliability is a measurement issue; pooling subpopulations does not test it.
Penetrance affects phenotype expression, not the structure-driven pooling discrepancy.
All regional samples are equal (2,000), so unequal sizes cannot drive the effect.

questions.stem: Repeating the Region C screen a decade later and obtaining an affected count statistically indistinguishable from the original most directly supports which Hardy-Weinberg assumption for that region?
options: Substantial migration occurred between Region C and the others
Allele and genotype frequencies remain constant across generations
The locus is subject to strong directional selection
Mating within the region is nonrandom
questions.explanation: A core prediction of Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium is that, absent evolutionary forces, allele and genotype frequencies remain constant from generation to generation. Finding the same affected (aa) frequency a decade later is consistent with this stability and supports the assumption that no major evolutionary force shifted q in Region C. Nonrandom mating and strong selection would tend to change genotype frequencies over time, so observed stability argues against them, not for them. Substantial migration would also alter allele frequencies; the steady result instead suggests migration was negligible. Thus the replication primarily provides evidence for generational constancy of frequencies.

distractors.why: Nonrandom mating shifts genotype frequencies; stability argues against it, not for it.
Strong selection would change q over time, contradicting the unchanged result.
Substantial migration would alter allele frequencies, inconsistent with the steady count.

questions.stem: Region D shows a markedly higher affected frequency than Region A. Given that the islands differ in founder size and consanguinity, which explanation is most consistent with the data and the disorder's inheritance?
options: The enzyme assay is more sensitive in Region D than in Region A
Region D has a higher mutation rate that converts a alleles to A alleles
A smaller founder population and higher consanguinity in Region D elevated the recessive allele frequency and homozygote production
Region D carriers express the disorder due to dominance of allele a
questions.explanation: The disorder is autosomal recessive, so only aa individuals are affected and carriers are unaffected, which eliminates the dominance explanation. A smaller founder population produces stronger genetic drift and founder effects that can raise an allele's frequency, and consanguineous mating increases homozygosity, both boosting the production of affected aa offspring. Region D's higher q (0.40 vs. 0.10 in Region A) and affected frequency are consistent with these forces. Differential assay sensitivity would be a measurement artifact, but the assay is described as validated and applied uniformly. A higher mutation rate converting a to A would lower, not raise, the recessive allele frequency, contradicting the observed increase.

distractors.why: Contradicts recessive inheritance; carriers are unaffected by definition here.
The assay is validated and applied uniformly, so sensitivity should not differ by region.
Converting a to A would reduce q, opposite to Region D's elevated allele frequency.

title: Fuel Switching During a Fasting Treadmill Test
text:
Whole-body fuel utilization can be estimated noninvasively using indirect calorimetry, which measures the volume of oxygen consumed (VO2) and carbon dioxide produced (VCO2). The respiratory exchange ratio (RER = VCO2/VO2) reflects the relative contribution of carbohydrate and fat to oxidative metabolism. Complete oxidation of glucose yields an RER of 1.0, whereas oxidation of a typical fatty acid yields an RER near 0.70, because fatty acids are more reduced and require more O2 per CO2 generated.

Investigators recruited 12 healthy, recreationally active adults to study how fasting duration affects substrate use during submaximal exercise. Each participant completed three identical 30-minute treadmill bouts at 50% of their predetermined maximal oxygen uptake, performed in randomized order on separate days after 4, 12, or 24 hours of fasting. Diet and sleep were standardized the day before each trial, and trials were separated by at least 72 hours. During the final 5 minutes of each bout, expired gases were collected and steady-state VO2, VCO2, and RER were recorded. Plasma was sampled immediately afterward for glucose, free fatty acids (FFA), and the ketone body beta-hydroxybutyrate. Results appear in Table 1.

As fasting duration increased, RER fell progressively, indicating a shift away from carbohydrate and toward fat oxidation. This was accompanied by rising plasma FFA and beta-hydroxybutyrate and a modest decline in plasma glucose. Total VO2 during exercise was similar across conditions, consistent with the matched workload. The investigators concluded that prolonged fasting promotes adipose lipolysis and hepatic fatty acid oxidation, sparing limited glycogen and glucose reserves. They noted that the design did not include a measure of muscle glycogen content and that participants served as their own controls across the three fasting states.

table.caption: Table 1. Steady-state respiratory and plasma measurements during 30 min of treadmill exercise at 50% VO2max after three fasting durations (means, n = 12).

questions.stem: Based on Table 1, which fasting condition reflects the greatest relative contribution of fat to oxidative metabolism?
options: All three were equal, because VO2 was unchanged
4-hour fast, because plasma glucose was highest
24-hour fast, because RER was lowest
12-hour fast, because FFA and glucose were intermediate
questions.explanation: RER (VCO2/VO2) approaches 1.0 when carbohydrate is the dominant fuel and approaches 0.70 when fat dominates, because fatty acids are highly reduced and consume more O2 per CO2 produced. The lowest RER therefore marks the greatest relative fat oxidation. In Table 1, RER falls from 0.91 (4 h) to 0.85 (12 h) to 0.78 (24 h), so the 24-hour fast shows the largest fat contribution. This matches the concurrent rise in plasma FFA (substrate) and beta-hydroxybutyrate (a product of hepatic fatty acid oxidation). Glucose level and unchanged VO2 do not indicate fuel mix; VO2 reflects total oxidative rate, not which substrate is burned.

distractors.why: Confuses high glucose with high fat oxidation; high glucose actually accompanies more carbohydrate use and a higher RER.
Picks the intermediate condition; intermediate values are not the maximum fat contribution.
Misreads unchanged VO2 (total oxidative rate, fixed by matched workload) as meaning fuel mix was identical.

questions.stem: The rise in plasma beta-hydroxybutyrate with longer fasting is most directly explained by increased:
options: Gluconeogenesis from glycerol in the liver
Pyruvate oxidation feeding the citric acid cycle
Hepatic ketogenesis from acetyl-CoA derived from fatty acid oxidation
Glycogenolysis in skeletal muscle
questions.explanation: Beta-hydroxybutyrate is a ketone body. During fasting, adipose lipolysis releases free fatty acids that undergo hepatic beta-oxidation, generating large amounts of acetyl-CoA. When oxaloacetate is diverted toward gluconeogenesis and acetyl-CoA exceeds citric acid cycle capacity, the liver condenses acetyl-CoA into ketone bodies (acetoacetate and beta-hydroxybutyrate). The data fit this: as fasting lengthens, FFA rises and beta-hydroxybutyrate rises in parallel. Muscle glycogenolysis produces glucose-6-phosphate for glycolysis, not ketones. Glycerol gluconeogenesis makes glucose, not ketone bodies. Increased pyruvate oxidation would reflect carbohydrate use (higher RER), the opposite of the observed trend.

distractors.why: Muscle glycogenolysis yields glucose-6-phosphate for ATP, not ketone bodies, and muscle lacks the enzymes for net ketogenesis.
Glycerol gluconeogenesis produces glucose; it does not generate ketone bodies.
Pyruvate oxidation indicates carbohydrate metabolism (higher RER), inconsistent with the falling RER and rising ketones.

questions.stem: Why did the investigators have each participant complete all three fasting conditions rather than assigning each person to a single condition?
options: To eliminate the need for a non-exercising control group
To increase the total sample size to 36 independent observations
To allow muscle glycogen to be measured indirectly
To use a within-subject design that controls for individual differences in fitness and metabolism
questions.explanation: This is a repeated-measures (within-subject, crossover) design: each participant serves as their own control across the three fasting states. Because baseline VO2max, body composition, and metabolic traits vary widely between people, having the same individuals experience every condition removes that between-person variability as a confounder, isolating the effect of fasting duration. The randomized order and 72-hour washout further guard against order and carryover effects. The trials are not 36 independent observations; they are correlated within person (n still 12). The design does not replace a control group conceptually, and it does nothing to measure glycogen, an acknowledged limitation.

distractors.why: Repeated measures on 12 people are not 36 independent observations; the trials are correlated within subject.
A within-subject design controls between-person variation; it does not address the absence of a resting control group.
The design gives no measure of muscle glycogen; the passage explicitly lists that as a limitation.

questions.stem: VO2 remained near 2.05 L/min across all three conditions. This observation primarily supports which conclusion?
options: Total energy expenditure during exercise was held roughly constant by matching workload
Carbohydrate oxidation was identical in all three conditions
Fat oxidation did not change across the fasting conditions
Participants reached maximal oxygen uptake in every trial
questions.explanation: VO2 reflects the total rate of oxidative ATP production, which scales with energy expenditure and was fixed here by exercising everyone at the same relative intensity (50% VO2max). The stable VO2 across conditions confirms the workload, and thus total oxidative demand, was successfully matched, an intended control. It does not reveal fuel mix; RER does that, and RER changed markedly. Because trials were at 50% of maximum, participants were not at VO2max. Carbohydrate and fat oxidation each shifted (RER fell), so neither was constant even though total VO2 was. Distinguishing total oxidative rate (VO2) from substrate selection (RER) is the key concept.

distractors.why: Constant VO2 says nothing about fuel mix; the falling RER shows fat oxidation actually increased.
Exercise was at 50% VO2max, so participants were well below maximal oxygen uptake.
The changing RER shows carbohydrate oxidation fell with fasting; total VO2 being constant does not make substrate use identical.

questions.stem: Which hormonal change best accounts for the combined pattern of rising plasma FFA, rising ketones, and falling glucose as fasting lengthens?
options: Decreased insulin with increased glucagon and catecholamines promoting lipolysis and ketogenesis
Unchanged hormone levels with effects driven purely by exercise
Increased insulin alone driving glucose uptake into adipose tissue
Increased insulin-to-glucagon ratio promoting fuel storage
questions.explanation: Prolonged fasting lowers blood glucose, which suppresses insulin and raises glucagon; exercise additionally raises catecholamines. A low insulin-to-glucagon ratio activates hormone-sensitive lipase in adipose tissue, driving lipolysis and the observed rise in plasma FFA. The delivered fatty acids feed hepatic beta-oxidation and ketogenesis, explaining the rise in beta-hydroxybutyrate, while reduced insulin and depleting glycogen contribute to the modest fall in glucose. A high insulin-to-glucagon ratio (option A) and insulin-driven adipose glucose uptake (option C) describe the fed state and would lower, not raise, FFA. Hormone levels clearly change across fasting durations, so the unchanged-hormone option is incorrect.

distractors.why: A high insulin/glucagon ratio favors storage and lipogenesis; it would lower FFA and ketones, opposite to the data.
Insulin promotes glucose uptake and inhibits lipolysis, so it cannot explain rising FFA and ketones during fasting.
Falling glucose during fasting necessarily shifts insulin and glucagon; hormones are not unchanged.

title: Encoding Depth and Word Recall
text:
Researchers examined how the type of processing performed during encoding affects later memory, building on the levels-of-processing framework, which holds that deeper, more meaningful (semantic) encoding produces stronger memory traces than shallow (structural or phonological) encoding.

Eighty undergraduates were randomly assigned to one of four orienting-task conditions (20 per condition). During the study phase, participants viewed 60 common nouns presented one at a time for 3 seconds each. For each word, they answered a yes/no question that determined how the word was processed. In the Structural condition, participants judged whether the word was printed in capital letters. In the Phonological condition, they judged whether the word rhymed with a cue word. In the Semantic condition, they judged whether the word fit meaningfully into a sentence frame. In the Self-Reference condition, they judged whether the word described themselves. Participants were not told their memory would be tested (incidental encoding).

After a 10-minute filler task of arithmetic problems, all participants completed a surprise free-recall test: they wrote down as many of the 60 studied words as they could in 5 minutes. The dependent variable was the number of words correctly recalled. The experimenter who scored recall was blind to condition assignment.

Mean recall increased across the four conditions in the predicted direction (Table 1). A one-way ANOVA revealed a significant effect of condition, F(3, 76) = 24.6, p < 0.001. Post-hoc comparisons showed that the Semantic and Self-Reference conditions each produced significantly more recall than the Structural and Phonological conditions, and the Self-Reference condition exceeded the Semantic condition. The Structural and Phonological conditions did not differ significantly from each other.

The researchers concluded that recall depends on how information is processed at encoding rather than on encoding time, since study duration was held constant across conditions. They interpreted the Self-Reference advantage as evidence that linking material to one's self-concept provides an especially elaborate, well-organized retrieval structure.

table.caption: Table 1. Mean number of words correctly recalled (out of 60) by orienting-task condition (n = 20 per condition).
rows: Does the word fit the sentence?

questions.stem: The study's design most directly tests which prediction of the levels-of-processing framework?
options: Information held in sensory memory decays within milliseconds unless attended to
Memory capacity in short-term storage is limited to roughly seven items
Retrieval is most successful when retrieval cues match the encoding context
Deeper, more semantic encoding produces stronger memory than shallow encoding
questions.explanation: The levels-of-processing framework proposes that memory durability depends on the depth of processing during encoding: shallow processing (structural features like capitalization, or phonological features like rhyme) yields weaker traces than deep semantic processing that engages meaning. The experiment manipulates exactly this by assigning different orienting tasks while holding study time constant, and recall rises from structural to phonological to semantic to self-reference (Table 1). Option A describes sensory memory decay (iconic/echoic), unrelated to the depth manipulation. Option C describes encoding specificity, a different principle not manipulated here. Option D describes Miller's short-term memory capacity, which the free-recall design does not test.

distractors.why: Confuses the depth-of-processing manipulation with sensory memory decay, a separate memory concept.
Names encoding specificity/cue-matching, which the design does not vary; cues were the same test for all.
Invokes Miller's 7±2 STM capacity, irrelevant to a 60-item long-term free-recall task.

questions.stem: Why did the experimenters hold the 3-second presentation time constant across all four conditions?
options: To create a longer delay before the free-recall test
To ensure participants reached the capacity limit of working memory
To increase the total number of words that could be presented in the study phase
To control study duration so any recall differences can be attributed to type of processing
questions.explanation: Holding presentation time constant controls a potential confound: if deeper conditions also allowed more study time, longer exposure rather than processing type could explain better recall. By fixing duration at 3 seconds for every condition, the only systematic difference across groups is the orienting task (independent variable), so recall differences can be attributed to depth of processing. This is the logic of controlling extraneous variables to preserve internal validity. Option A misstates the purpose; total words (60) is fixed regardless. Option C is unrelated; the manipulation is not about reaching a capacity limit. Option D is wrong because the delay was the separate 10-minute filler task, not the presentation time.

distractors.why: Misreads constant timing as a way to fit more items, not as confound control.
Introduces working-memory capacity, which constant timing does not address.
Confuses presentation time with the 10-minute retention interval (filler task).

questions.stem: Based on Table 1, approximately what percentage of the 60 studied words did participants in the Self-Reference condition recall on average?
options: About 36%
About 27%
About 46%
About 15%
questions.explanation: The Self-Reference condition had a mean recall of 27.4 words out of 60. Converting to a percentage: 27.4 / 60 = 0.457, or about 46%. This requires reading the correct cell of Table 1 and normalizing to the 60-item list length given in the passage and caption. Option B (about 27%) is the trap of reporting the raw mean (27.4) as if it were already a percentage. Option A (about 15%) is the Structural condition's percentage (9.2/60 = 15%), the wrong row. Option C (about 36%) is the Semantic condition's percentage (21.8/60 = 36%), an adjacent but incorrect row. Always divide the correct condition mean by the total list length (60).

distractors.why: Uses the Structural mean (9.2/60 = 15%) instead of the Self-Reference mean.
Reports the raw mean (27.4) as a percentage without dividing by 60.
Uses the Semantic mean (21.8/60 = 36%), an adjacent but incorrect row.

questions.stem: The significant ANOVA, F(3, 76) = 24.6, p < 0.001, together with the post-hoc results, best supports which conclusion?
options: The Structural and Phonological conditions produced the strongest memory
At least one condition mean differed from the others, and deeper processing conditions outperformed shallow ones
Differences among the means were likely due to chance
Every condition differed significantly from every other condition
questions.explanation: A significant omnibus ANOVA indicates that not all group means are equal, but it does not by itself specify which pairs differ; that requires post-hoc tests. The passage reports that Semantic and Self-Reference each exceeded Structural and Phonological, and Self-Reference exceeded Semantic, consistent with deeper processing producing more recall. Option A is false because the passage explicitly states Structural and Phonological did not differ significantly. Option C contradicts Table 1, where those two shallow conditions had the lowest means. Option D misreads p < 0.001; such a small p value indicates the differences are unlikely to be due to chance, so the null hypothesis is rejected.

distractors.why: Overstates results; Structural vs. Phonological was explicitly nonsignificant.
Contradicts Table 1, where shallow conditions had the lowest recall means.
Misinterprets a tiny p value; p < 0.001 means chance is an unlikely explanation.

questions.stem: The superior recall in the Self-Reference condition is best explained by which memory principle?
options: Elaborative encoding that integrates items with an organized self-schema
State-dependent retrieval based on matching internal mood states
Proactive interference from previously learned material
Maintenance rehearsal that keeps items active in short-term memory
questions.explanation: Relating words to oneself forces elaborative encoding: each item is linked to a rich, highly organized self-schema, creating numerous meaningful associations and retrieval routes, which strengthens and organizes the memory trace (the self-reference effect). This explains why Self-Reference exceeded even Semantic processing in Table 1. Option A, proactive interference, would impair recall, the opposite of the finding. Option C, maintenance rehearsal, is shallow rote repetition that produces weaker long-term memory than elaboration and was not what the orienting task encouraged. Option D, state-dependent retrieval, concerns matching internal states at encoding and retrieval, which the design neither manipulated nor described.

distractors.why: Proactive interference hurts memory; it cannot explain superior recall.
Maintenance rehearsal is shallow and yields weaker long-term memory, not the best recall.
State-dependent retrieval was not manipulated; mood states were not varied or matched.

title: Detecting Tones in Noise
text:
Signal detection theory distinguishes an observer's perceptual sensitivity from the decision criterion they adopt when responding under uncertainty. Researchers examined how background noise and monetary incentives jointly shape auditory detection. On each trial, a faint 1000-Hz pure tone was either presented or withheld against a continuous white-noise background, and participants pressed one key for "tone present" and another for "tone absent."

Forty young adults with normal hearing completed 400 trials across four blocks defined by a 2 x 2 design. Noise level was manipulated within subjects as low (45 dB) or high (60 dB) background intensity; the physical tone intensity was held constant at 50 dB. Payoff structure was also manipulated within subjects: in the liberal-payoff blocks, participants earned bonus money for every hit (correctly reporting a present tone) and were not penalized for false alarms (reporting a tone when none occurred); in the conservative-payoff blocks, false alarms incurred a monetary penalty while hits earned the same bonus. Block order was counterbalanced across participants.

For each block the experimenters computed the hit rate, the false-alarm rate, the sensitivity index d-prime (larger values indicate better discrimination of signal from noise), and the criterion. Detection sensitivity was expected to depend on the physical signal-to-noise relationship, whereas response bias was expected to track the payoff structure. Mean reaction time on correct trials was also recorded.

Results appear in Table 1. Raising the background noise lowered d-prime, consistent with a degraded signal-to-noise ratio at the level of the receptor and afferent pathway. Shifting from conservative to liberal payoffs raised both the hit rate and the false-alarm rate within each noise condition. Across all blocks, d-prime tracked noise level while the hit and false-alarm rates shifted together with payoff, illustrating the theoretical separation of sensitivity from bias. Reaction times were modestly faster under liberal payoffs, suggesting a lower evidence threshold for responding "present."

table.caption: Table 1. Mean detection performance by noise level and payoff structure (n = 40).

questions.stem: Based on Table 1, which manipulation most strongly affected sensitivity (d-prime) rather than response bias?
options: Block order
Background noise level
Payoff structure
Reaction-time instructions
questions.explanation: Within each payoff condition, d-prime drops sharply as noise rises from 45 to 60 dB (e.g., 2.10 to 1.20 under conservative payoffs and 2.05 to 1.15 under liberal payoffs), while d-prime barely changes between payoff conditions at a fixed noise level. In signal detection theory, d-prime indexes perceptual sensitivity, which depends on the physical signal-to-noise ratio. Payoff structure instead shifts the hit and false-alarm rates together, changing the criterion (bias) without altering sensitivity. So noise is the manipulation that drives d-prime. Block order was counterbalanced (a control), and no reaction-time instruction was given, so those choices do not explain the sensitivity differences.

distractors.why: Confuses bias with sensitivity: payoff shifts the criterion (hit and false-alarm rates move together) but leaves d-prime nearly constant at a given noise level.
Block order was counterbalanced as a control to cancel order effects, not a manipulated variable affecting d-prime.
No reaction-time instruction was manipulated; RT was only measured, so it cannot be the cause of sensitivity differences.

questions.stem: A reviewer worries that participants may have simply pressed 'present' more often in liberal blocks because they expected reward, not because they perceived the tone differently. Which feature of the design best addresses whether sensitivity truly differed across conditions?
options: Computing d-prime, which is independent of the response criterion
Counterbalancing block order across participants
Holding the tone intensity constant at 50 dB
Recruiting only participants with normal hearing
questions.explanation: The reviewer's concern is about response bias: a willingness to say 'present' more often. d-prime is constructed to separate true perceptual sensitivity from this decision tendency, because it is computed from both the hit rate and the false-alarm rate. A liberal criterion inflates hits but also false alarms, so d-prime stays roughly constant if real sensitivity is unchanged. Thus reporting d-prime directly answers whether discrimination differed beyond mere bias. Counterbalancing controls order effects, normal-hearing screening reduces between-subject variability, and a constant tone level ensures equal physical signal, but none of these mathematically disentangles sensitivity from criterion the way d-prime does.

distractors.why: Counterbalancing controls practice and fatigue order effects; it does not separate perceptual sensitivity from response bias.
Normal-hearing screening improves sample homogeneity but does nothing to distinguish sensitivity from a willingness to say 'present'.
A constant tone level equates the physical signal but, on its own, cannot reveal whether response differences reflect bias versus sensitivity.

questions.stem: In the high-noise, liberal-payoff block, the hit rate is high but so is the false-alarm rate. This pattern is best described as the participant adopting:
options: A liberal criterion with relatively low sensitivity
A conservative criterion with high sensitivity
A liberal criterion with high sensitivity
An unbiased criterion with high sensitivity
questions.explanation: A liberal criterion means the observer requires little evidence before responding 'present,' which raises both the hit rate and the false-alarm rate, exactly as the liberal payoff produced. Sensitivity, however, is set by noise: in the high-noise block d-prime falls to about 1.15, much lower than the roughly 2.0 seen in low noise. So this block combines a liberal criterion (from payoff) with relatively low sensitivity (from high noise). A conservative criterion would instead suppress both hits and false alarms, and high sensitivity would require a large d-prime, which the high-noise data do not show.

distractors.why: A conservative criterion lowers false alarms; the high false-alarm rate shows the opposite, and high noise lowers, not raises, sensitivity.
An unbiased criterion would not inflate the false-alarm rate, and high noise yields low, not high, sensitivity.
The criterion is correctly identified as liberal, but high noise reduces d-prime, so sensitivity is low, not high.

questions.stem: Raising the background noise from 45 dB to 60 dB lowered d-prime. This finding is most consistent with which concept from sensation and perception?
options: Sensory adaptation eliminating the receptor response entirely
Weber's law predicting a constant just-noticeable difference ratio
An increase in the absolute threshold and a worsened signal-to-noise ratio
Top-down processing overriding sensory input
questions.explanation: Louder background noise raises the level of stimulation needed to reliably detect the tone, effectively increasing the absolute threshold for that signal and degrading the signal-to-noise ratio reaching the auditory receptors and afferent pathway. With a weaker effective signal relative to noise, discrimination of present versus absent trials worsens, lowering d-prime. This is a bottom-up, stimulus-driven effect on detectability. Top-down processing concerns expectations and context, not the physical degradation here. Weber's law describes proportional difference thresholds between two suprathreshold stimuli, not noise-limited detection. Sensory adaptation is a decline in response to a constant stimulus over time and would not selectively abolish the receptor response as described.

distractors.why: Top-down processing involves expectation and context; the change here is a physical, bottom-up degradation of the stimulus.
Weber's law concerns the ratio for discriminating two suprathreshold stimuli, not detection of a signal buried in noise.
Adaptation is a time-dependent decline to a constant stimulus; it does not describe a noise-driven rise in detection threshold.

questions.stem: Which of the following is the clearest dependent variable in this study?
options: Background noise level
Payoff structure
The 2 x 2 within-subjects design
The sensitivity index d-prime
questions.explanation: A dependent variable is the measured outcome that the experimenters expect to change in response to their manipulations. Here the researchers manipulated noise level and payoff structure (the independent variables) and then measured outcomes such as hit rate, false-alarm rate, reaction time, and the sensitivity index d-prime. d-prime is computed from the data, not set by the experimenter, so it is a dependent variable. Noise level and payoff structure are the independent variables that were deliberately set across blocks. The 2 x 2 within-subjects description is the experimental design itself, not a variable that is measured. Therefore d-prime is the clearest dependent variable among the choices.

distractors.why: Background noise level was deliberately set by the experimenters, making it an independent variable, not a measured outcome.
Payoff structure was manipulated across blocks, so it is an independent variable rather than a dependent measure.
A 2 x 2 within-subjects design names the structure of the study; it is not a measured variable at all.

title: Conformity Under Vocal Dissent
text:
Researchers examined how social influence shapes publicly stated judgments on an ambiguous perceptual task. Undergraduate participants (N = 240) viewed pairs of dot patterns and estimated which contained more dots; the correct answer was deliberately near-impossible to determine. Each real participant was seated with several confederates who answered aloud first, before the participant gave a verbal response. Participants were randomly assigned to one of four conditions that varied the social context (see Table 1).

In the Control condition, no confederates were present and participants answered privately. In the Unanimous condition, all six confederates gave the same predetermined (incorrect) estimate before the participant responded. In the Lone Ally condition, five confederates gave the incorrect estimate but one confederate consistently gave a different, accurate estimate. In the Anonymous condition, six confederates again gave the unanimous incorrect estimate, but participants recorded their answers privately on a keypad rather than stating them aloud.

The investigators measured the conformity rate, defined as the proportion of trials on which a participant matched the confederates' incorrect estimate. They also administered a post-task questionnaire assessing whether participants privately believed the group estimate was correct. After the session, participants were debriefed and told the true purpose of the study.

As shown in Table 1, conformity was lowest in the Control condition and highest when the majority was unanimous and responses were public. The presence of a single dissenting ally and the option to respond anonymously each reduced conformity substantially. On the questionnaire, the large majority of conforming participants reported that they did not actually believe the group's estimate but went along with it anyway. The researchers concluded that public agreement on an ambiguous task is driven more by social pressure than by genuine attitude change.

table.caption: Table 1. Conformity rate and private belief by experimental condition.

questions.stem: The pattern of results across conditions is most consistent with which type of social influence?
options: Obedience, because participants followed orders from an authority figure
Internalization, because participants permanently adopted the group's belief
Informational social influence, because participants used the group as a source of accurate information
Normative social influence, because conformity dropped sharply when responses were made privately
questions.explanation: Normative social influence occurs when people conform to gain acceptance or avoid disapproval, producing public compliance without private belief change. The data fit this: in the Anonymous condition (private responses), conformity fell from 37% to 13%, and the questionnaire showed only about 8-9% of conformers actually believed the group. If influence were informational (using the group as a knowledge source on a genuinely ambiguous task), removing the public audience would not so dramatically reduce conformity, and private belief should track behavior. Internalization implies lasting genuine belief change, contradicted by the low 'believed group' percentages. Obedience involves an authority issuing commands, which is absent here; the confederates were peers.

distractors.why: Tempting because the task is ambiguous, but informational influence predicts conformity persists when private and that beliefs change, which the data contradict.
Internalization means genuine, lasting acceptance; the questionnaire shows conformers did not believe the group, so this is wrong.
Obedience requires an authority figure giving directives; the influence here comes from peer confederates, not authority.

questions.stem: Why did the researchers include the Control condition?
options: To serve as the independent variable in the experiment
To control for the effect of the post-task questionnaire
To increase the sample size and improve statistical power
To establish a baseline error rate against which conformity in social conditions can be compared
questions.explanation: The Control condition has no confederates and private responses, so any selection of the 'incorrect' estimate reflects only the task's inherent difficulty, not social pressure. Its 4% rate provides a baseline error rate. Comparing the social conditions (37%, 11%, 13%) against this 4% shows how much of the conformity is attributable to social influence rather than guessing on an ambiguous task. Without this baseline, an elevated rate could not be confidently attributed to the manipulation. The condition is not the independent variable (the IV is the social-context manipulation, which has levels); it does not boost power per se; and it does not control for questionnaire effects.

distractors.why: A control condition's purpose is comparison, not adding power; power depends on per-group sample size and effect size.
Conditions are levels of the independent variable, not the IV itself; the control is one such level serving as baseline.
The questionnaire is the same across conditions and is not what the control isolates; the control isolates task difficulty.

questions.stem: Based on Table 1, the introduction of a single accurate ally reduced the conformity rate by approximately what proportion relative to the Unanimous condition?
options: About 11%
About 26%
About 70%
About 50%
questions.explanation: Conformity was 37% in the Unanimous condition and 11% in the Lone Ally condition. The absolute drop is 37 - 11 = 26 percentage points. As a proportion of the Unanimous baseline, 26/37 is approximately 0.70, or about a 70% reduction. The question asks for the proportional reduction relative to the unanimous group, so the correct choice is about 70%. The 26% option confuses the absolute percentage-point drop with the proportional change. The 11% option simply restates the Lone Ally value. The 50% option underestimates the proportional decrease. This mirrors classic findings that breaking unanimity, even with one dissenter, dramatically undermines conformity pressure.

distractors.why: 11% is just the Lone Ally conformity rate, not a reduction; it ignores the comparison to 37%.
26 is the absolute percentage-point drop, not the proportional reduction the question requests.
50% understates the change; 26/37 is about 70%, not half.

questions.stem: A critic argues the study cannot determine whether anonymity OR the absence of a visible audience caused the lower conformity in the Anonymous condition. This criticism best identifies a problem with:
options: The reliability of the conformity measure
Random assignment of participants
The external validity of the dependent variable
A confound between two simultaneously changed factors
questions.explanation: The Anonymous condition changed the response mode to private keypad entry, which simultaneously removed the public audience and provided anonymity. Because these two factors covary perfectly across the manipulation, the design cannot isolate which one drove the reduction. That is the definition of a confound: an extraneous variable that changes alongside the independent variable, making causal attribution ambiguous. It is not a problem of external validity (generalizing to other settings), not a failure of random assignment (participants were randomly assigned), and not a reliability issue (the conformity measure could be perfectly consistent yet still confounded). Isolating the factors would require separate conditions varying audience and anonymity independently.

distractors.why: External validity concerns generalization; the issue here is internal, about which factor caused the effect.
Random assignment was performed and is unrelated; the problem is two factors bundled within one condition.
Reliability is measurement consistency; a confound can exist even with a perfectly reliable measure.

questions.stem: The post-task finding that most conforming participants did not privately believe the group's estimate best illustrates which distinction?
options: The difference between a stereotype and prejudice
The difference between an in-group and an out-group
The difference between the foot-in-the-door and door-in-the-face techniques
The difference between compliance and internalization
questions.explanation: Compliance is outward agreement with a group while privately maintaining one's own view, whereas internalization is genuine private acceptance of the group's position. Conformers who changed their public answers but reported not believing the group exemplify compliance, not internalization. This maps directly onto the questionnaire data (only ~8-9% believed the group). The stereotype/prejudice distinction concerns cognitive beliefs versus affective attitudes about groups, irrelevant here. Foot-in-the-door and door-in-the-face are sequential-request persuasion techniques, not present in this design. In-group versus out-group concerns social categorization and identity, not the public-versus-private agreement at issue. The compliance/internalization contrast is the core conceptual point of the study.

distractors.why: Stereotype vs. prejudice is about belief vs. emotion toward groups, unrelated to public vs. private agreement.
These are sequential persuasion techniques; no escalating or shrinking requests were used in this study.
In-group/out-group is about social categorization, not the compliance-without-belief pattern shown.

title: Education, Neighborhoods, and Hypertension
text:
Researchers examined how socioeconomic status (SES) relates to cardiovascular health across a large U.S. metropolitan area. They recruited 4,800 adults aged 40-65 from neighborhoods that varied widely in median household income. Each participant completed a survey reporting educational attainment, household income, and self-rated chronic stress (a validated 0-30 scale, higher = more stress). Trained nurses, blind to participants' survey responses, measured resting blood pressure using a standardized protocol; hypertension was defined as systolic pressure of at least 140 mmHg or current use of antihypertensive medication.

The team's central hypothesis drew on the fundamental cause theory of health disparities, which holds that higher-SES individuals can deploy flexible resources—knowledge, money, prestige, and beneficial social connections—to avoid disease risks regardless of the specific mechanisms operating in a given era. They predicted that hypertension prevalence would decline as educational attainment rose, and that this gradient would persist even after accounting for income.

Participants were grouped into four education strata. For each stratum, the researchers recorded hypertension prevalence, mean chronic-stress score, and the proportion living in a neighborhood lacking a full-service grocery store (a "food desert"). Results appear in Table 1.

The investigators observed a clear inverse social gradient: each successive increase in education was associated with lower hypertension prevalence, lower mean stress, and reduced exposure to food deserts. Even within income brackets, more-educated participants showed lower hypertension prevalence, a pattern the authors interpreted as consistent with fundamental cause theory rather than a purely material explanation.

Because the data were cross-sectional, the authors cautioned that causal direction could not be firmly established and that unmeasured factors—such as early-life adversity or access to preventive care—might contribute to the observed associations. They called for longitudinal follow-up to clarify whether educational interventions could narrow cardiovascular disparities over time.

table.caption: Table 1. Hypertension prevalence, mean chronic-stress score, and food-desert exposure by educational attainment (N = 4,800)

questions.stem: The researchers' prediction that the hypertension gradient would persist 'even after accounting for income' most directly reflects which theoretical claim?
options: That material deprivation is the sole driver of health disparities
That social mobility eliminates differences in health outcomes between strata
That flexible, non-material resources tied to high SES help people avoid disease regardless of mechanism
That hypertension is primarily genetically determined within social classes
questions.explanation: Fundamental cause theory argues that higher-SES individuals use flexible resources—knowledge, money, prestige, and social connections—to avoid health risks no matter the specific disease mechanism. Predicting that the education gradient survives statistical control for income isolates the non-material resources (knowledge, prestige, networks) that education confers, showing health advantage is not reducible to money alone. Option A states the opposite (pure materialism). Option C invokes genetics, which the passage never tests and which fundamental cause theory does not require. Option D contradicts the persistent gradient the authors found and predicted. Thus the prediction maps onto the claim that non-material SES resources protect health across mechanisms.

distractors.why: Controlling for income is meant to show disparities are NOT solely material, the opposite of this option.
Genetics is never measured or invoked; fundamental cause theory emphasizes social resources, not heredity.
The persistence of the gradient shows differences remain, contradicting the claim that mobility erases them.

questions.stem: Having nurses who were blind to participants' survey responses measure blood pressure most directly served to reduce which threat to the study's validity?
options: Selection bias in recruiting participants
Observer (measurement) bias in the outcome
Confounding by household income
Attrition over the follow-up period
questions.explanation: Blinding the outcome assessors to predictor information prevents their expectations from systematically influencing measurement—an observer or measurement bias. If nurses knew a participant's education or stress score, they might unconsciously record blood pressure differently, inflating the apparent gradient. Keeping them blind protects the objectivity of the outcome. Selection bias concerns who enters the study, addressed by recruitment, not measurement blinding. Confounding by income is handled through statistical adjustment, not blinding. Attrition applies to longitudinal designs; this study is cross-sectional, so there is no follow-up period from which participants could drop out. Therefore blinding specifically targets observer bias in the blood-pressure measurement.

distractors.why: Selection bias arises during recruitment, not during outcome measurement, so blinding nurses does not address it.
Income confounding is handled by statistical control, not by blinding the person taking the measurement.
Attrition requires longitudinal follow-up; this is a cross-sectional study with no repeated measurement.

questions.stem: Using Table 1, compare the absolute difference in hypertension prevalence between the lowest and highest education strata with the absolute difference in food-desert exposure between those same strata. Which statement is correct?
options: The hypertension difference (~31.6 points) is slightly larger than the food-desert difference (~26.5 points)
The two differences are essentially equal (~26 points each)
The hypertension difference (~26.5 points) is slightly smaller than the food-desert difference (~31.6 points)
The hypertension difference is roughly twice the food-desert difference
questions.explanation: From Table 1, hypertension prevalence falls from 48.6% (less than high school) to 22.1% (bachelor's or higher), an absolute difference of 26.5 percentage points. Food-desert exposure falls from 41.0% to 9.4%, a difference of 31.6 percentage points. So the hypertension gap (~26.5) is slightly smaller than the food-desert gap (~31.6), making option A correct. Option B swaps the two magnitudes and reverses the direction. Option C is wrong because the gaps differ by about 5 points (26.5 vs 31.6), not equal. Option D is wrong because twice 31.6 would be ~63, far above the 26.5-point hypertension gap; the hypertension gap is actually smaller, not larger.

distractors.why: Swaps the magnitudes: 31.6 is the food-desert gap and 26.5 the hypertension gap, and it reverses the direction.
The gaps differ by ~5 points (26.5 vs 31.6), so they are not essentially equal.
Twice 31.6 is ~63; the hypertension gap is 26.5, which is smaller than—not double—the food-desert gap.

questions.stem: The authors note that because the data were cross-sectional, causal direction 'could not be firmly established.' Which alternative interpretation does this caveat most directly leave open?
options: That the sample was too small to detect any association
That poorer cardiovascular health could limit educational attainment, rather than the reverse
That the blood-pressure measurements were unreliable
That hypertension and education are statistically independent
questions.explanation: Cross-sectional designs measure exposure and outcome at one time point, so they cannot establish temporal order. The caveat leaves open reverse causation: poor cardiovascular health (or its early determinants) could constrain how much education a person attains, rather than education shaping later blood pressure. This is the classic limitation the authors flag. Option B concerns measurement reliability, which standardized protocols and blinding addressed, not the cross-sectional issue. Option C is wrong because N = 4,800 is large and clear gradients emerged, indicating ample power. Option D contradicts the data, which show a strong inverse association across strata. Thus the design caveat specifically admits the possibility of reverse causality.

distractors.why: Reliability is a measurement issue addressed by the standardized protocol, unrelated to temporal ordering.
With N = 4,800 and clear gradients, the study is well powered; small sample is not the concern.
The table shows a strong inverse gradient, so the variables are clearly associated, not independent.

questions.stem: The consistent worsening of hypertension, stress, and food-desert exposure as one moves down the education strata is best described by which concept?
options: The sick role
Spurious correlation
Cultural capital
A social gradient in health
questions.explanation: A social gradient in health refers to the stepwise pattern in which health outcomes improve at each successive rung up the socioeconomic ladder, not merely a poor-versus-rich dichotomy. Table 1 shows exactly this: prevalence and stress decline and grocery access improves monotonically from the lowest to highest education stratum. The sick role (Parsons) describes social expectations of ill individuals, irrelevant here. Spurious correlation implies the association is driven entirely by an outside variable with no real link, but the authors argue for a meaningful SES-health relationship. Cultural capital denotes non-financial social assets like education and tastes; while related to SES, it does not name the graded health pattern itself. Hence 'social gradient in health' fits best.

distractors.why: The sick role describes behavioral expectations of ill people, not a graded SES-health pattern.
Spurious correlation implies no true relationship; the authors interpret the gradient as a genuine SES effect.
Cultural capital is an SES resource, but it does not name the stepwise health pattern shown across strata.

title: Praise and Persistence in Children
text:
Researchers examined how different forms of adult feedback shape children's motivation and persistence after failure. The study drew on a distinction between praise directed at a stable trait ("You're so smart") and praise directed at effort or strategy ("You worked really hard"). Trait praise was hypothesized to foster a fixed mindset, in which ability is seen as unchangeable, whereas process praise was hypothesized to foster a growth mindset, in which ability is seen as malleable through effort.

A total of 160 children were tested individually. Each child first completed a set of easy puzzles and received one of three feedback types: trait praise, process praise, or neutral feedback ("You finished"). The neutral group served as a control. Children were then given a second, deliberately very difficult set of puzzles on which nearly all children failed. Finally, every child completed a third set matched in difficulty to the first set.

The study used a 3 (feedback) x 2 (age: 5-year-olds vs. 9-year-olds) between-subjects design, with children randomly assigned to feedback condition within each age group. After the difficult set, researchers recorded two outcomes: persistence, measured as minutes spent voluntarily attempting additional optional puzzles, and performance change, measured as the difference in puzzles solved between the third set and the first set (a negative value indicates a drop).

Researchers also collected a self-report measure of task enjoyment on a 1-7 scale. To reduce experimenter bias, the adult delivering feedback was blind to the study's hypotheses, and all puzzles were pre-tested for difficulty. Outcomes are summarized in Table 1. The investigators concluded that process praise buffered children against the negative effects of failure, particularly among older children, who showed the largest divergence between feedback conditions. They cautioned that effects on 5-year-olds were weaker and that enjoyment ratings did not differ reliably across conditions.

table.caption: Table 1. Mean persistence, performance change, and enjoyment by feedback condition and age (SD in parentheses).

questions.stem: According to the passage, trait praise was hypothesized to foster a fixed mindset. Which belief best characterizes a child with a fixed mindset?
options: Failure provides useful information for adjusting strategy
Intelligence is a stable quality that cannot be substantially changed
Ability depends largely on the difficulty of the task
Effort is the main route to improving one's ability
questions.explanation: A fixed mindset, as described by Dweck's work, is the belief that core abilities such as intelligence are stable and largely unchangeable. Trait praise ('You're so smart') ties success to an enduring quality, so failure threatens that self-image and reduces persistence, consistent with the trait-praise group's largest performance drop. Option A describes a growth mindset (effort drives improvement). Option C also reflects growth-mindset reasoning, treating failure as informative rather than threatening. Option D misattributes ability to external task difficulty rather than to a belief about the self, which is not the mindset construct. Only option B captures the defining belief that ability is fixed.

distractors.why: Describes a growth mindset (effort improves ability), the opposite of fixed.
Reflects growth-mindset framing of failure as informative feedback.
Externalizes ability to task difficulty rather than capturing a belief about self.

questions.stem: Why did the researchers include a neutral feedback group ('You finished')?
options: To serve as a control baseline against which praise effects could be compared
To increase the total sample size for statistical power
To manipulate the difficulty of the second puzzle set
To measure the children's baseline intelligence before the study
questions.explanation: The neutral group received feedback with no trait or process content, providing a baseline that isolates the effect of praise type. Without it, researchers could not tell whether process praise raised persistence or trait praise lowered it relative to no manipulation. The data support this: process praise (8.6 min) exceeds neutral (5.2 min) and trait praise (3.1 min) falls below it in 9-year-olds, showing effects in both directions. Option A confuses a control condition with a sample-size strategy. Option C describes a separate experimental manipulation, not the purpose of the control. Option D is wrong because the neutral feedback came after a task, not as an intelligence pretest.

distractors.why: Confuses the logical role of a control with merely adding participants.
The difficulty manipulation was the hard second set, not the neutral group.
Neutral feedback was delivered after a task, not as a baseline IQ measure.

questions.stem: Based on Table 1, which comparison most directly supports the conclusion that process praise buffered children against failure-related performance declines among 9-year-olds?
options: Process praise (9 yr) enjoyment exceeded trait praise (9 yr) enjoyment
Process praise (9 yr) persistence exceeded process praise (5 yr) persistence
Neutral (9 yr) performance change was less negative than trait praise (5 yr)
Process praise (9 yr) showed +0.9 performance change versus -2.4 for trait praise (9 yr)
questions.explanation: Buffering against failure-related decline is measured by the performance change variable. Among 9-year-olds, process praise produced a gain (+0.9 puzzles) while trait praise produced the largest drop (-2.4), a clear divergence supporting the conclusion. Option A uses enjoyment, which the passage states did not differ reliably and does not index performance. Option B compares persistence across ages, addressing an age effect rather than buffering of performance. Option D compares across both age and condition (neutral 9 yr vs. trait 5 yr), confounding the two factors and not isolating the process-vs-trait contrast within 9-year-olds. Only option C contrasts the relevant outcome within the same age group.

distractors.why: Uses enjoyment, which the passage says did not differ reliably.
Compares persistence across ages, an age effect, not buffering of performance.
Mixes age and condition, confounding the comparison.

questions.stem: The persistence outcome (minutes spent on optional puzzles after failure) is best described as an operationalization of which motivational concept?
options: Extrinsic motivation driven by an external reward
Drive reduction following a physiological need
Intrinsic motivation reflected in voluntary task engagement
Incentive salience produced by a conditioned cue
questions.explanation: Time spent voluntarily attempting optional puzzles, with no reward offered, operationalizes intrinsic motivation: engaging in an activity for its inherent interest rather than an external payoff. Option A is incorrect because no external reward (money, prizes) was provided; the puzzles were optional. Option C, drive-reduction theory, explains behavior aimed at restoring physiological homeostasis (hunger, thirst), which puzzle persistence does not involve. Option D, incentive salience, refers to a conditioned cue acquiring motivational 'wanting,' typically in reward-learning contexts, and does not fit voluntary cognitive engagement. The optional, unrewarded nature of the continued effort is the key feature pointing to intrinsic motivation.

distractors.why: No external reward was offered; the puzzles were unrewarded and optional.
Drive reduction concerns physiological needs, not voluntary cognitive effort.
Incentive salience involves conditioned reward cues, not free engagement.

questions.stem: A critic argues that because the same adult delivered feedback to all children, the results might reflect that adult's behavior rather than the feedback type itself. Which study feature most directly addresses this concern?
options: Pre-testing all puzzles for difficulty before the study
Including two age groups in a between-subjects design
Random assignment of children to feedback condition within each age group
Keeping the feedback-delivering adult blind to the study's hypotheses
questions.explanation: The concern is experimenter bias: an adult who knows the hypotheses might unintentionally signal expectations and shape outcomes. Keeping that adult blind to the hypotheses directly limits this, because they cannot systematically nudge children toward predicted results. Option A (random assignment) controls for pre-existing differences between children but does not stop a single experimenter from behaving differently across conditions. Option C (pre-testing difficulty) ensures puzzle sets are comparable but says nothing about the experimenter's conduct. Option D (age groups) addresses generalizability across development, not experimenter influence. The blinding procedure is the specific safeguard matched to the critic's objection.

distractors.why: Controls participant differences, not the experimenter's biased behavior.
Equalizes task difficulty but does not address experimenter conduct.
Addresses developmental generalizability, not experimenter bias.

title: Treating Insomnia: CBT-I vs. Pharmacotherapy
text:
Chronic insomnia is often treated either pharmacologically or behaviorally. Cognitive-behavioral therapy for insomnia (CBT-I) targets maladaptive sleep cognitions and behaviors, combining stimulus control, sleep restriction, and cognitive restructuring. Researchers compared CBT-I to a common sedative-hypnotic and to their combination, hypothesizing that the behavioral components would produce more durable gains because they address the conditioned arousal and dysfunctional beliefs that maintain insomnia.

Adults (N = 240) meeting diagnostic criteria for chronic primary insomnia were randomly assigned to one of four conditions: CBT-I alone, hypnotic medication alone, CBT-I plus medication, or a pill-placebo control. All participants kept daily sleep diaries. The treatment phase lasted 8 weeks. The primary outcome was sleep-onset latency (SOL), the minutes taken to fall asleep after lights-out, with lower values indicating improvement. SOL was recorded at baseline, at the end of the 8-week treatment phase (post-treatment), and at a 6-month follow-up after all active treatment had stopped.

To reduce expectancy effects, the placebo and medication pills were visually identical, and outcome assessors who scored the diaries were blind to condition assignment. Therapists delivering CBT-I could not be blinded to whether a participant received behavioral therapy. The four groups did not differ significantly on baseline SOL, age, or insomnia duration.

Table 1 presents mean SOL (in minutes) at each time point. At post-treatment, all three active conditions showed lower SOL than placebo. However, the conditions diverged at follow-up: the medication-alone group's SOL rose substantially after the drug was discontinued, approaching its baseline, whereas the CBT-I group largely maintained its gains. The combination group showed intermediate durability. The investigators concluded that, although adding medication accelerated early symptom relief, the behavioral intervention conferred the most durable long-term benefit, consistent with the view that learning-based mechanisms underlie lasting change.

table.caption: Table 1. Mean sleep-onset latency (minutes) by condition and time point.

questions.stem: Which feature of the study design most directly controls for participant expectancy (placebo) effects in evaluating the medication?
options: Keeping daily sleep diaries throughout the study
Use of visually identical placebo and medication pills
Random assignment of participants to the four conditions
Measuring sleep-onset latency at three time points
questions.explanation: Expectancy (placebo) effects arise when participants' beliefs about receiving an active treatment alter outcomes. Making the placebo and medication pills visually identical blinds participants to which pill they received, so any difference between the medication and placebo groups cannot be attributed to participants merely believing they got a drug. Random assignment controls for pre-existing group differences and confounds, not expectancy specifically. Sleep diaries and repeated measurement are data-collection tools; they record outcomes but do nothing to equalize participant beliefs. Thus identical pills is the design element that isolates the drug's pharmacological effect from expectancy.

distractors.why: Confuses random assignment (controls baseline confounds/selection) with blinding (controls expectancy).
Sleep diaries are a measurement tool; they record outcomes but do not equalize participant beliefs.
Repeated measurement tracks change over time but does not address expectancy effects.

questions.stem: A reviewer notes that therapists delivering CBT-I could not be blinded to whether a participant received behavioral therapy. This unblinding is best described as a potential threat to:
options: The external validity of the sample
The reliability of the sleep-onset latency measure
Internal validity, via experimenter expectancy effects
The random assignment of participants
questions.explanation: When experimenters know which condition a participant is in, their expectations can subtly influence behavior and outcomes (experimenter expectancy or Rosenthal effects), introducing a confound that threatens internal validity, the extent to which the observed effect can be attributed to the treatment itself. Random assignment already occurred and is unaffected by later therapist knowledge. Reliability concerns measurement consistency, but the diaries were scored by blinded assessors, so the SOL measure's reliability is protected. External validity concerns generalizability to other populations, which therapist blinding does not bear on. Hence the unblinded therapist is an internal-validity threat through expectancy.

distractors.why: Random assignment happened before treatment and is not undone by therapist knowledge.
Outcome scoring was done by blinded assessors, so measure reliability is safeguarded.
External validity is about generalizing to other samples, not about therapist blinding.

questions.stem: Based on Table 1, which condition produced the greatest reduction in mean SOL from baseline to 6-month follow-up?
options: CBT-I + medication
Medication alone
CBT-I alone
Placebo
questions.explanation: The change from baseline to follow-up is what indicates durable benefit. CBT-I alone fell from 62 to 29 minutes, a 33-minute reduction. Medication alone went from 61 to 55, only a 6-minute reduction, because gains were lost after the drug stopped. The combination dropped from 63 to 38, a 25-minute reduction, intermediate in durability. Placebo went from 60 to 57, a 3-minute change. Therefore CBT-I alone shows the largest sustained decrease at follow-up. Note that the active drug conditions had very low post-treatment SOL (medication 24, combination 22) but did not fully maintain it, illustrating why the relevant comparison is the baseline-to-follow-up change, not the post-treatment value.

distractors.why: Tempting because medication had a strong post-treatment value (24), but SOL rebounded to 55 at follow-up.
Combination improved durably (25 min) but less than CBT-I alone (33 min).
Placebo barely changed (60 to 57), the smallest reduction of all.

questions.stem: Which comparison in Table 1 most strongly supports the conclusion that medication's benefit was not durable once treatment stopped?
options: CBT-I follow-up SOL (29) versus combination follow-up SOL (38)
Combination baseline SOL (63) versus CBT-I baseline SOL (62)
Medication-alone follow-up SOL (55) versus its own baseline SOL (61)
Medication-alone post-treatment SOL (24) versus placebo post-treatment SOL (53)
questions.explanation: Durability is assessed by asking whether gains achieved during treatment persist after it ends. Comparing the medication group's 6-month follow-up SOL (55) to its own baseline (61) shows the value returned nearly to where it started, demonstrating that the early improvement was lost once the drug was discontinued. The medication-versus-placebo post-treatment contrast shows the drug worked initially but says nothing about durability. The CBT-I-versus-combination follow-up contrast compares two other conditions, not medication's persistence. The baseline-versus-baseline comparison merely confirms groups started similarly. The within-group baseline-to-follow-up change is the direct evidence of relapse.
